question: [File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Can an unprivileged market creator deploy a malicious IOracle that always reverts on price(), then create a market with that oracle as CollateralParams.oracle, causing any call to liquidate() that iterates the collateralBitmap while loop (Midnight.sol:607-618) to revert for every borrower who has activated that collateral, permanently blocking liquidation of all undercollateralized positions in that market at zero ongoing cost? Preconditions: market created permissionlessly with attacker-controlled oracle address; at least one borrower has non-zero collateral[i] and collateralBitmap bit i set. Call sequence: (1) attacker deploys RevertOracle; (2) attacker calls touchMarket with CollateralParams[i].oracle = RevertOracle; (3) victim borrower calls supplyCollateral(market, i, assets, borrower) — oracle NOT called here; (4) victim takes sell offer, debt increases; (5) price drops, position becomes unhealthy; (6) liquidator calls liquidate() → IOracle(RevertOracle).price() reverts → liquidate reverts. Invariant tested: unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable. Scoped impact: permanent liquidation DoS at attacker cost of one contract deployment + one touchMarket call. Proof idea: deploy RevertOracle, create market, supply collateral, open debt, drop price, assert liquidate always reverts regardless of collateralIndex or input amounts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / isHealthy / take] Can an unprivileged seller with debt activate a collateral whose oracle reverts, causing every subsequent take() call against any of their sell offers to revert at the final isHealthy check (Midnight.sol:476), effectively freezing all taker-side liquidity for that seller's offers at the cost of one supplyCollateral call with 1 wei? Preconditions: market has multi-collateral params; seller has existing debt; seller calls supplyCollateral for collateral index j whose oracle is currently non-reverting, then the oracle begins reverting (or seller activates a collateral with a known-to-revert oracle). Call sequence: (1) seller opens debt via take; (2) seller calls supplyCollateral(market, j, 1, seller) activating collateral j; (3) oracle at CollateralParams[j].oracle starts reverting; (4) any taker calls take(offer_where_seller_is_seller, ...) → isHealthy(market, id, seller) → IOracle.price() reverts → take reverts. Invariant tested: take must complete for valid offers with healthy sellers. Scoped impact: DoS on take for all offers where victim is seller, at cost of 1 wei collateral deposit. Proof idea: fuzz collateral index j, assert take always reverts after oracle for j is made to revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Can an unprivileged borrower in a multi-collateral market (up to 16 activated collaterals per MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER) activate one collateral whose oracle is a gas-exhaustion contract, causing liquidate() to run out of gas when iterating the collateralBitmap while loop, permanently blocking liquidation of their undercollateralized position? Preconditions: market has at least two collateral params; borrower activates collateral i (healthy oracle) and collateral j (gas-exhaustion oracle); borrower has debt. Call sequence: (1) borrower supplies collateral i and j; (2) borrower opens debt; (3) position becomes unhealthy; (4) liquidator calls liquidate() with any collateralIndex → while loop hits j → IOracle(gasExhaustOracle).price() consumes all gas → OOG revert. Invariant tested: unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable. Scoped impact: permanent liquidation DoS via gas exhaustion oracle at cost of 1 wei collateral deposit. Proof idea: deploy GasExhaustOracle (infinite loop), assert liquidate OOGs for all collateralIndex values.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] If a malicious oracle returns 0 for the liquidated collateral (liquidatedCollatPrice = 0), does the liquidate() path with repaidUnits > 0 revert due to division by zero at seizedAssets = repaidUnits.mulDivDown(lif, WAD).mulDivDown(ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE, liquidatedCollatPrice) (Midnight.sol:652), and does the path with seizedAssets > 0 silently compute repaidUnits = 0, allowing collateral seizure with zero debt repayment, creating bad debt? Preconditions: oracle for collateralIndex returns 0; borrower is unhealthy; liquidator calls liquidate with repaidUnits > 0 or seizedAssets > 0. Call sequence: (1) deploy ZeroOracle; (2) create market with ZeroOracle for collateral 0; (3) borrower supplies collateral 0 and opens debt; (4) liquidator calls liquidate(market, 0, 0, repaidUnits=1, ...) → division by zero revert; (5) liquidator calls liquidate(market, 0, seizedAssets=1, 0, ...) → repaidUnits = 0 → collateral seized, debt unchanged. Invariant tested: liquidation must repay correct debt proportional to seized collateral. Scoped impact: DoS on repaidUnits path; silent bad-debt creation on seizedAssets path. Proof idea: assert liquidate(repaidUnits>0) reverts and liquidate(seizedAssets>0) leaves debt unchanged.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / isHealthy / take] Does the isHealthy check at Midnight.sol:476 call IOracle.price() for every bit set in the seller's collateralBitmap, meaning that if the seller has activated 16 collaterals (MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER) each with a moderately expensive oracle, the gas cost of take() can be driven high enough to exceed block gas limits or make takes economically unviable, constituting a low-cost griefing DoS? Preconditions: seller activates 16 collaterals; each oracle performs expensive computation (e.g., Chainlink aggregator with many sources). Call sequence: (1) seller supplies 16 collaterals; (2) seller opens debt; (3) taker calls take() → isHealthy iterates 16 oracle calls → gas cost spikes. Invariant tested: take must be executable at reasonable gas cost. Scoped impact: economic DoS on take for sellers with many activated collaterals. Proof idea: fuzz number of activated collaterals 1..16, measure gas cost of take, assert it stays below a reasonable threshold.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Can an oracle that returns type(uint256).max cause an arithmetic overflow in the mulDivDown call at Midnight.sol:613 (_collateral.mulDivDown(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE)) when _collateral is large (e.g., type(uint128).max), since mulDivDown computes (x * y) / d using unchecked Solidity 0.8 multiplication that will revert on overflow, permanently blocking liquidate() for that borrower? Preconditions: oracle returns type(uint256).max; borrower has collateral[i] = type(uint128).max; position is unhealthy. Call sequence: (1) deploy MaxOracle returning type(uint256).max; (2) create market; (3) borrower supplies type(uint128).max collateral; (4) oracle returns max; (5) liquidator calls liquidate() → _collateral * price overflows uint256 → revert. Invariant tested: unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable regardless of oracle return value within uint256 range. Scoped impact: permanent liquidation DoS via oracle overflow. Proof idea: fuzz (collateral, price) pairs where collateral * price > type(uint256).max, assert liquidate reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / isHealthy] Can an oracle returning type(uint256).max cause the same overflow in isHealthy (Midnight.sol:954) during the take() seller health check, blocking all take() calls against a seller with debt and large collateral, even if the seller is actually healthy? Preconditions: oracle returns type(uint256).max; seller has debt and collateral[i] > 0; taker calls take with seller as seller. Call sequence: (1) seller supplies collateral; (2) seller opens debt via take; (3) oracle starts returning type(uint256).max; (4) taker calls take() → isHealthy → _position.collateral[i].mulDivDown(type(uint256).max, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE) overflows → revert. Invariant tested: take must succeed for valid offers with healthy sellers. Scoped impact: DoS on take for all sell offers from affected seller. Proof idea: unit test with collateral = 1e18, price = type(uint256).max, assert isHealthy reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] In the liquidate() bad-debt realization loop (Midnight.sol:607-618), the oracle is called for ALL activated collaterals regardless of which collateralIndex is being liquidated. Can an attacker who controls one collateral's oracle (e.g., as market creator) selectively revert that oracle only when a liquidation is attempted, blocking bad-debt realization and preventing the lossFactor update that would socialize losses among lenders, thereby freezing lender credit redemptions indirectly? Preconditions: market has two collaterals; attacker controls oracle for collateral 1; borrower has activated both collaterals; borrower has bad debt. Call sequence: (1) attacker creates market with MaliciousOracle for collateral 1; (2) borrower activates both collaterals and opens debt; (3) position becomes undercollateralized (bad debt); (4) liquidator calls liquidate() → oracle for collateral 1 reverts → bad debt never realized → lossFactor never updated → lenders cannot correctly redeem. Invariant tested: bad debt must be realizable; lossFactor must be updatable. Scoped impact: DoS on bad-debt realization and indirect freeze of lender credit. Proof idea: assert liquidate always reverts when one oracle in the bitmap reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / isHealthy / withdrawCollateral] Can an oracle that reverts block withdrawCollateral() for a borrower with debt (Midnight.sol:568 calls isHealthy which calls oracle), even if the borrower wants to add more collateral first and then withdraw a different collateral, effectively freezing the borrower's collateral management? Preconditions: borrower has debt; one activated collateral's oracle reverts; borrower wants to withdraw a different collateral that would still leave position healthy. Call sequence: (1) borrower activates collateral i (good oracle) and collateral j (reverting oracle); (2) borrower opens debt; (3) borrower calls withdrawCollateral(market, i, assets, borrower, receiver) → isHealthy → oracle for j reverts → revert. Invariant tested: borrower must be able to manage collateral composition. Scoped impact: DoS on withdrawCollateral for borrowers with any reverting oracle in their bitmap. Proof idea: assert withdrawCollateral reverts even when withdrawn collateral's oracle is healthy.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Does the liquidate() function call IOracle.price() for the collateralIndex being liquidated to set liquidatedCollatPrice (Midnight.sol:611), and separately iterate ALL activated collaterals for maxDebt/badDebt computation (Midnight.sol:607-618), meaning that even a liquidation targeting collateral i will revert if any other activated collateral j has a reverting oracle, even though j is not being seized? Preconditions: borrower has activated collaterals i and j; oracle for j reverts; liquidator wants to liquidate collateral i only. Call sequence: (1) borrower activates i and j; (2) oracle for j starts reverting; (3) liquidator calls liquidate(market, collateralIndex=i, ...) → while loop hits j → oracle for j reverts → revert. Invariant tested: liquidation of one collateral must not be blocked by unrelated collateral's oracle. Scoped impact: DoS on liquidation even when target collateral oracle is healthy. Proof idea: assert liquidate(collateralIndex=i) reverts when oracle for j (j≠i) reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Can a borrower front-run a liquidation by calling supplyCollateral(market, j, 1, self) to activate a collateral j whose oracle is known to revert (since supplyCollateral does NOT call oracle per Midnight.sol:524-546), thereby making their position permanently unliquidatable at the cost of 1 wei of collateral token j? Preconditions: market has collateral j with reverting oracle; borrower has debt and is about to be liquidated; borrower holds 1 wei of collateral token j. Call sequence: (1) borrower's position becomes unhealthy; (2) liquidator submits liquidate() tx; (3) borrower front-runs with supplyCollateral(market, j, 1, self) → collateralBitmap bit j set, no oracle call; (4) liquidator's liquidate() tx executes → while loop hits j → oracle reverts → liquidate reverts. Invariant tested: unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable; supplyCollateral must not be usable to block liquidation. Scoped impact: permanent liquidation DoS via front-run at cost of 1 wei. Proof idea: state test showing supplyCollateral with reverting oracle succeeds, then liquidate fails.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] If a borrower activates a collateral j with a reverting oracle and then calls liquidate(market, j, 0, 0, borrower, ...) (the zero-input bad-debt realization path documented at Midnight.sol:577-579), does the while loop still call oracle for j and revert, or does the zero-input path skip the oracle call, and if it skips, can the borrower use this to selectively realize bad debt without triggering the oracle revert? Preconditions: borrower has bad debt; collateral j oracle reverts; borrower calls liquidate with seizedAssets=0 and repaidUnits=0. Call sequence: (1) borrower activates j with reverting oracle; (2) borrower has bad debt; (3) anyone calls liquidate(market, j, 0, 0, borrower, ...) → while loop still iterates all bitmap bits including j → oracle reverts → revert. Invariant tested: zero-input liquidation for bad-debt realization must be executable. Scoped impact: DoS on bad-debt realization path. Proof idea: assert liquidate(0,0) reverts when any activated oracle reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Can a borrower who has activated a collateral with a reverting oracle use the liquidation lock mechanism (LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT, Midnight.sol:444/475) to compound the DoS: since liquidationLocked returns true during take callbacks, and the NotLiquidatable check at Midnight.sol:620-624 passes when liquidationLocked is true, does this create a window where the borrower is both unliquidatable (oracle reverts) and lock-protected, and can this state persist beyond the callback window? Preconditions: borrower has debt; activated oracle reverts; borrower is seller in a take with callback. Call sequence: (1) borrower is seller; (2) taker calls take() → liquidationLocked set to true for seller; (3) during callback, liquidator tries liquidate() → liquidationLocked=true → NotLiquidatable passes but oracle still reverts in while loop → revert; (4) after take, lock cleared but oracle still reverts. Invariant tested: liquidation lock must not extend DoS window beyond callback. Scoped impact: compounded DoS during take callback window. Proof idea: assert liquidate reverts during and after take callback when oracle reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / isHealthy / take] Does the take() function call isHealthy for the seller only when liquidationLocked(id, seller) is false (Midnight.sol:476: require(liquidationLocked(id, seller) || isHealthy(...))), meaning that if the seller is already in a take callback (locked), a nested take against the same seller skips the oracle call entirely, and can an attacker exploit this to take a sell offer from a seller whose oracle would otherwise revert, bypassing the health check? Preconditions: seller has debt; seller's oracle reverts; seller is already in a take callback (liquidationLocked=true). Call sequence: (1) outer take sets liquidationLocked for seller; (2) during outer take's callback, attacker calls inner take against same seller → liquidationLocked=true → isHealthy skipped → oracle not called → inner take succeeds even if seller is unhealthy. Invariant tested: seller health must be verified on every take unless locked by protocol design. Scoped impact: potential health check bypass during nested take. Proof idea: assert inner take during callback does not call oracle and verify seller health post-take.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] In the post-maturity liquidation mode, the LIF ramps from 1 to maxLif over TIME_TO_MAX_LIF (15 minutes, ConstantsLib.sol:19). If the oracle for the liquidated collateral reverts during this 15-minute window, is the entire post-maturity liquidation blocked, and can a borrower deploy a time-based oracle that reverts only during the first 15 minutes post-maturity to prevent profitable liquidations while allowing unprofitable ones later? Preconditions: market has passed maturity; borrower has debt; oracle reverts for block.timestamp in [maturity, maturity + 15 minutes]. Call sequence: (1) market matures; (2) liquidator calls liquidate(postMaturityMode=true) within 15 minutes → oracle reverts → revert; (3) after 15 minutes, oracle returns valid price → liquidation succeeds but LIF is now maxLif (less profitable for liquidator). Invariant tested: post-maturity liquidation must be executable throughout the ramp window. Scoped impact: DoS on profitable post-maturity liquidations. Proof idea: deploy TimedRevertOracle, assert liquidate reverts in [maturity, maturity+15min] and succeeds after.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Can a borrower with LLTV=1e18 (the special LLTV=1 case, Midnight.sol:22-26) and a reverting oracle still block liquidation, given that LLTV=1 has no overcollateralization and the RCF is inactive, meaning the protocol relies entirely on oracle-based liquidation to unwind positions? Preconditions: market has LLTV=1 for collateral i; borrower has debt; oracle for i reverts. Call sequence: (1) create market with LLTV=1; (2) borrower supplies collateral i and opens debt; (3) oracle starts reverting; (4) liquidator calls liquidate() → oracle reverts → revert; (5) no alternative liquidation path exists since LLTV=1 means no overcollateralization. Invariant tested: LLTV=1 markets must still be liquidatable. Scoped impact: permanent liquidation DoS in LLTV=1 markets. Proof idea: assert liquidate always reverts for LLTV=1 market when oracle reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Does the badDebt computation in liquidate() (Midnight.sol:614-616) use mulDivUp(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE) and mulDivUp(WAD, maxLif), and if the oracle returns a value such that _collateral * price overflows uint256 in this mulDivUp call (which uses (x*y + d-1)/d), does this cause a different overflow revert path than the maxDebt computation, and can an attacker craft a collateral amount and oracle price that overflows badDebt but not maxDebt (or vice versa) to selectively block liquidation? Preconditions: oracle returns value P; collateral amount C such that C*P overflows uint256 in mulDivUp but not mulDivDown (or vice versa). Call sequence: (1) deploy oracle returning P; (2) borrower supplies C collateral; (3) liquidator calls liquidate() → mulDivUp(C, P, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE) overflows → revert. Invariant tested: liquidation arithmetic must not overflow for any valid (collateral, price) combination. Scoped impact: DoS on liquidation via arithmetic overflow in badDebt path. Proof idea: fuzz (C, P) pairs near overflow boundary, assert liquidate reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / isHealthy / take] Can a market creator create a market where the oracle for collateral i is a contract that calls back into Midnight (reentrancy via IOracle.price()), and does this reentrant call corrupt the partial state of take() or isHealthy() before the seller health check completes, causing either a false health result or a state inconsistency? Preconditions: oracle for collateral i calls back into Midnight.take() or Midnight.liquidate() during price(); seller has debt and collateral i activated. Call sequence: (1) deploy ReentrantOracle that calls Midnight.take() in price(); (2) taker calls take() → isHealthy → oracle.price() → reentrant take() → partial state from outer take visible → potential inconsistency. Invariant tested: oracle price() must not be able to reenter Midnight and observe or corrupt partial state. Scoped impact: state corruption or false health check via oracle reentrancy. Proof idea: deploy ReentrantOracle, assert inner take sees consistent state and outer take completes correctly.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] In the liquidate() while loop, the oracle is called for each bit in collateralBitmap using msb() to find the highest set bit (Midnight.sol:608). If a borrower has activated all 16 allowed collaterals (MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER=16) and each oracle is a moderately expensive view function, does the cumulative gas cost of 16 oracle calls plus the bitmap iteration make liquidate() exceed the block gas limit for reasonable collateral counts, constituting a gas-based DoS? Preconditions: borrower activates 16 collaterals; each oracle costs ~50k gas; total liquidate gas > 30M. Call sequence: (1) borrower supplies 16 collaterals; (2) borrower opens debt; (3) position becomes unhealthy; (4) liquidator calls liquidate() → 16 oracle calls → OOG. Invariant tested: liquidation must be executable within block gas limits. Scoped impact: gas-based DoS on liquidation for borrowers with many collaterals. Proof idea: measure gas cost of liquidate with 16 expensive oracles, assert it stays below block gas limit.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / isHealthy] Does isHealthy() (Midnight.sol:944-960) return true (healthy) when debt=0 without calling any oracle, meaning that a borrower who has repaid all debt can always withdraw collateral regardless of oracle state, and conversely, does a borrower with non-zero debt but zero collateralBitmap (all collaterals cleared) have maxDebt=0 and thus isHealthy returns false (0 >= debt fails for debt>0), blocking withdrawCollateral even though there is no collateral to value? Preconditions: borrower has debt > 0 and collateralBitmap = 0 (all collaterals withdrawn). Call sequence: (1) borrower opens debt; (2) borrower withdraws all collateral (each withdrawal calls isHealthy); (3) final state: debt>0, bitmap=0; (4) isHealthy returns maxDebt=0 >= debt=false → unhealthy. Invariant tested: isHealthy must correctly handle zero-bitmap with non-zero debt. Scoped impact: verify no DoS path exists for zero-bitmap borrowers. Proof idea: assert isHealthy(debt>0, bitmap=0) returns false and verify this is the intended behavior.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Can a borrower exploit the fact that supplyCollateral() does not call the oracle (Midnight.sol:524-546) to activate a collateral with a known-to-revert oracle in a market they did not create, using only a publicly available market with a collateral whose oracle has become broken (e.g., deprecated Chainlink feed), thereby making their position permanently unliquidatable in an otherwise legitimate market? Preconditions: legitimate market exists with collateral j whose oracle has become broken/reverting; borrower has debt in that market. Call sequence: (1) legitimate market exists; (2) oracle for collateral j starts reverting (e.g., deprecated feed); (3) borrower calls supplyCollateral(market, j, 1, self) → no oracle call → collateral j activated; (4) liquidator calls liquidate() → oracle for j reverts → revert. Invariant tested: oracle liveness must be enforced at collateral activation time. Scoped impact: DoS on liquidation via activation of broken oracle collateral. Proof idea: assert supplyCollateral succeeds with reverting oracle, then liquidate fails.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / claimContinuousFee] Does claimContinuousFee() (Midnight.sol:312-325) call IOracle.price() at any point, and if not, can oracle DoS ever block fee claiming? Conversely, does claimSettlementFee() (Midnight.sol:305-310) call oracle? If neither calls oracle, verify that fee claiming is oracle-independent and cannot be DoS'd via oracle manipulation, and identify any indirect path (e.g., via continuousFeeCredit accounting that depends on lossFactor which depends on liquidation which depends on oracle) that could freeze claimable fees. Preconditions: market has accrued continuous fees; oracle for a collateral reverts. Call sequence: (1) lenders accrue credit; (2) continuous fee accrues to continuousFeeCredit; (3) oracle starts reverting; (4) feeClaimer calls claimContinuousFee() → no oracle call → succeeds. Invariant tested: fee claiming must be oracle-independent. Scoped impact: verify claimContinuousFee and claimSettlementFee are not DoS-able via oracle. Proof idea: assert both claim functions succeed regardless of oracle state.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / repay] Does repay() (Midnight.sol:502-521) call IOracle.price() at any point, and if not, can a borrower always repay debt regardless of oracle state? Verify that the repay callback path (IRepayCallback.onRepay) cannot be used by an attacker to inject an oracle-dependent revert, and that the safeTransferFrom call is the only external call that could block repay. Preconditions: borrower has debt; oracle for activated collateral reverts; borrower calls repay with no callback. Call sequence: (1) borrower opens debt; (2) oracle starts reverting; (3) borrower calls repay(market, units, onBehalf, address(0), '') → no oracle call → debt decremented → withdrawable incremented → safeTransferFrom → succeeds. Invariant tested: repay must be oracle-independent. Scoped impact: verify repay cannot be DoS'd via oracle. Proof idea: assert repay succeeds with reverting oracle when callback is address(0).

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / withdraw] Does withdraw() (Midnight.sol:481-500) call IOracle.price() at any point, and if not, can a lender always withdraw credit regardless of oracle state? Verify that _updatePosition (called in withdraw) does not call oracle, and that the only external call is safeTransfer. Preconditions: lender has credit; oracle for a collateral in the market reverts; lender calls withdraw. Call sequence: (1) lender has credit; (2) oracle starts reverting; (3) lender calls withdraw(market, units, onBehalf, receiver) → touchMarket (no oracle) → _updatePosition (no oracle) → credit decremented → safeTransfer → succeeds. Invariant tested: withdraw must be oracle-independent. Scoped impact: verify withdraw cannot be DoS'd via oracle. Proof idea: assert withdraw succeeds with reverting oracle.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IOracle.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IOracle.price / liquidate] Can an oracle that returns a value P such that _collateral.mulDivDown(P, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivDown(lltv, WAD) causes maxDebt to overflow uint256 (via accumulation across multiple collaterals in the while loop) block liquidate() with an arithmetic overflow, even though each individual collateral's contribution is within uint256 range? Preconditions: borrower has 16 activated collaterals each with large collateral amounts; oracle returns large P for each; sum of contributions overflows uint256. Call sequence: (1) borrower activates 16 collaterals with large amounts; (2) oracle returns large P; (3) liquidator calls liquidate() → maxDebt accumulation overflows uint256 → revert. Invariant tested: maxDebt accumulation must not overflow for any valid multi-collateral configuration. Scoped impact: DoS on liquidation via maxDebt overflow. Proof idea: fuzz 16 (collateral, price) pairs, assert maxDebt accumulation stays within uint256.
